coredata.dc:title: A computational analysis of effects of electrode thickness on the energy density of lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, F.M. NizamUddin
Rasul, Mohammad G.
Sayem, A.S.M.
Mandal, Nirmal K.
coredata.dc:description: Elevated energy density is a prime requirement for many lithium-ion battery (LIB) applications, including electric vehicles (EVs). At the cell level, the enhanced energy density of LIBs is achievable by designing thicker electrodes, which decreases the weight of the inactive materials. This work proposed a reduced order electrochemical model (ROEM), which is an extended single particle model (extended SPM), by incorporating the existing order reduction techniques of Pseudo-Two-Dimensional (P2D) model. The proposed model was used to predict the energy density values of a nickel cobalt manganese (NCM) cell with respect to the cathode thickness. The validation of the model was performed by using MATLAB at 0.05C, 1C, 2C, 3C and 5C rates by comparing the simulation results with the experimental data obtained from the Stanford Energy Control Laboratory. The verified model was then used to simulate the effect of varying thicknesses of the cathode on energy density of the NCM cell at C/5, C/2, 1C, and 2C rates of discharge. The simulation results illustrate that increasing the cathode thickness has a profound positive impact on the energy density of the cell. The prediction of the model demonstrates a total of around 20 % increase in energy density for the increment of cathode thickness from 75 μm to 600 μm. The proposed model can replicate energy density for thicker electrodes up to 2C rate reasonably correctly. However, the incorporation of thermal characteristics and other influential parameters of LIB cells into the proposed model can yield a better prediction of energy density of thicker electrodes at higher C-rates, which is recommended for further study.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: CAUL institutions 2023: Hybrid journals full funding
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Reduced order electrochemical model
Model validity
Energy density predictions
Thicker electrodes
Increasing energy density

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge and thank Fund for Accessing Potential Expertise (FAPEx) for providing financial support as FAPEx 2022 grant under the title “Design and optimization of lithium-ion battery as an efficient energy storage device for electric vehicles” to support this research.

head.ce:title: A computational analysis of effects of electrode thickness on the energy density of lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering and Technology, Central Queensland University, North Rockhampton, Queensland, 4701, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering and Technology,Central Queensland University, North Rockhampton, Queensland 4701, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departmentof Mechanical Engineering, Chittagong University of Engineering Technology, Chattogram, Bangladesh
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departmentof Mechanical Engineering,Chittagong University of Engineering Technology, Chattogram, Bangladesh

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Elevated energy density is a prime requirement for many lithium-ion battery (LIB) applications, including electric vehicles (EVs). At the cell level, the enhanced energy density of LIBs is achievable by designing thicker electrodes, which decreases the weight of the inactive materials. This work proposed a reduced order electrochemical model (ROEM), which is an extended single particle model (extended SPM), by incorporating the existing order reduction techniques of Pseudo-Two-Dimensional (P2D) model. The proposed model was used to predict the energy density values of a nickel cobalt manganese (NCM) cell with respect to the cathode thickness. The validation of the model was performed by using MATLAB at 0.05C, 1C, 2C, 3C and 5C rates by comparing the simulation results with the experimental data obtained from the Stanford Energy Control Laboratory. The verified model was then used to simulate the effect of varying thicknesses of the cathode on energy density of the NCM cell at C/5, C/2, 1C, and 2C rates of discharge. The simulation results illustrate that increasing the cathode thickness has a profound positive impact on the energy density of the cell. The prediction of the model demonstrates a total of around 20 % increase in energy density for the increment of cathode thickness from 75 μm to 600 μm. The proposed model can replicate energy density for thicker electrodes up to 2C rate reasonably correctly. However, the incorporation of thermal characteristics and other influential parameters of LIB cells into the proposed model can yield a better prediction of energy density of thicker electrodes at higher C-rates, which is recommended for further study.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: LIBs have been robustly used in many fields as novel energy storage devices due to their outstanding features. The common applications include mobile phones, portable computers, and electric cars because of their high-efficiency, environment friendliness, and convenience to store renewable energy [1]. Moreover, some attractive features of LIBs such as high energy density, high power density, long life cycle, low self-discharge, a wide range of operating temperatures, and the lack of memory effect etc. have made them notable and widespread in their applications [2,3]. Currently, the area of applications for LIBs is expanding at an accelerated pace to engulf military and aerospace usage [4]. Hence, LIBs have been explored from both the materials’ perspective and parametric design perspective over the last few decades to achieve certain application-oriented features [5–7]. In parallel to the materials-based design, which consists of fabrication, modification, and optimization of internal components of LIBs by using various materials, their alloys, and composite materials, cell parameter-based design and engineering have also been proven to be equally important [8].

Modelling of LIBs and numerical simulations of those models have become a widely accepted alternative to the experimental processes due to the cost, time and resources involved in conducting experimental research [9]. In addition, most of the experimental studies have been performed in a limited environment at the cell level without having the flexibility of presenting different scenarios. On the other hand, both the cell level and the system level complex electrochemical mechanisms of LIBs can be replicated through numerical studies, including the representation of practical applications [10]. However, different types of models have been developed so far based on the complexities and the fields of applications. Battery models are classified mainly in accordance with the physical insights such as white box models, grey box models, and black box models as well as the parameter estimation strategy such as electrochemical models, mathematical models, equivalent circuit models, and data-driven models. Among the models, electrochemical models have been studied widely and extensively because of their ability to predict battery cell chemistry precisely. A set of nonlinear differential equations are used to represent the chemical reactions that occur inside the battery in electrochemical modelling [11].

Two most popular and extensively used and tested electrochemical models for LIBs are the P2D model and the SPM. There are three aspects of P2D model namely as the theories related to porous electrode and concentrated solution as well as the equations representing the kinetics of the cell. The accuracy of the P2D model is much higher and the results from the model are more aligned to experimental data. However, the P2D model involves a lot of parameters, many partial differential equations (PDEs), ordinary differential equations, and algebraic equations, yielding higher accuracy but increasing the computational burden and complexity in simulation. For example, when PDEs for electrodes and separator in P2D model are discretised, a total of 600 differential algebraic equations needs to be solved simultaneously for the entire cell. To avoid the complexity of P2D models and to achieve enhanced efficiency in modelling and simulation, SPMs have been introduced. Moreover, SPMs use some assumptions, and have fewer equations and parameters, leading to computational ease and higher efficiency but with compromised accuracy [11–13]. SPMs can be improved by considering electrolyte dynamics to attain better accuracy at higher C-rates, which is a reduced order electrochemical model, known as SPMe [14]. Hence, model order reduction through applying different numerical methods along with various algorithms has been undertaken by researchers as an important task in battery modelling [15]. A detailed review of different types of available LIB models can be found in Ref. [16].

The development of an appropriate model to represent the cell performance based on design parameters such as the thickness, porosity, and particle size of electrodes is important as the performance of LIBs is highly dependent on cell-level parameters. Three types of cell parameters influence the performance of LIBs-i) Design parameters such as electrode thickness, porosity, particle radius, and volume fraction of active materials, etc. ii) Kinetic parameters such as equilibrium voltage, transfer coefficient for electrode current, etc. and iii) Transport parameters such as ionic conductivity, Li-ion diffusion coefficient, and Li-ion transference number etc. The performance of LIBs was improved by studying the design parameters of the P2D model that had been presented by Newman [17]. A reformulated model was used to simulate and optimize LIB cell design variables like electrode thickness and porosity simultaneously with an aim to increase the specific energy [18]. The influence of particle size of LIBs on the thermal and electrochemical performance was studied by using Newman's P2D model [19]. Huebner et al. investigated the effect of thickness and porosity of LIB cathode of an NCM-622 cell on its electrochemical performance with the help of a mathematical model. They found a moderate increase in the energy density of the cell with the increase of electrode thickness and the decrease of electrode porosity [20]. The electrode thickness effect on electrochemical performance and power and energy density of LIB cells was investigated respectively by Danner et al. and Zhang et al. [17].

So far, many studies have been performed to demonstrate the impact of electrode thickness on the specific energy of LIB cells as described briefly in section 2. However, most of them has done so through the analysis of electrochemical performances of LIB cells. Drawing direct relationship between the electrode thickness and the energy density of a LIB cell, and the illustrations of the variation of the energy density with respect to the electrode thickness has not been evident in the existing studies. There are only few illustrations that have shown the relationship between energy density and electrode thickness directly as in Refs. [21–23] and that too have been done for optimization purposes with only showing the initial and final results before and after optimization, excluding the depictions of the relationships in the intermediate steps. This study has addressed the issues with an attempt to cover up the gaps in the study by illustrating direct relationship between electrode thickness and energy density of LIB cells in a total of 5 steps, which has made the trend of the relationship more evident than ever. To attain the target, a ROEM, which has also been termed as an extended SPM, has been formulated based on both the simple SPM model and the model order reduction of P2D model from the existing literature. The proposed model has been verified with the experimental data collected from open-source online datasets provided by Stanford Energy Control Laboratory.

The organization of the paper is as follows: after highlighting the interrelationship between electrode thickness and energy density in section 2, the physics-based electrochemical models are described in section 3 with reduced order models, including the SPM. Section 4 presents the simulation methodology, including the proposed ROEM, which is followed by section 5 focusing on experimental data source, engulfing experimental procedures and data collection methods. The findings of the study with relevant explanations have been presented in the result and discussion section 6 and finally, a conclusion is drawn in section 7, mentioning recommendations for future study regarding the subject matter of this paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode thickness vs energy density of LIB cells
ce:section.ce:para: Energy density of LIBs is significantly influenced by the electrode thickness, with an increased value of energy density for thicker electrodes, which can be achieved through the enhancement of active materials in the electrodes and simultaneously reducing the fraction of inactive materials such as separator and current collectors. Energy density (volumetric) of an NMC LIB cell was found to be increased to 1200 WhL−1 from 800 WhL−1 with the increase in cathode thickness from 24 μm to 108 μm and that of a Lithium-ferro phosphate (LFP) cell has been increased to 700 WhL−1 from 550 WhL−1 with electrode thickness increase from 25 μm to 108 μm with a compromise of power density. That improvement in energy density was reported as a result of aluminium (current collector) volume fraction reduction, indicating a possibility of clearer picture of more improvement if other inactive materials such as separator and packaging elements were considered [8]. Up to 29 % increase in energy density was found with respect to the increase in the thickness from 70 μm to 305 μm in NMC. The findings came through an experimental investigation with enhanced kinetics loss and decreased cycling stability for thicker electrodes [24]. A rough calculation showed that much higher energy density than its present values in LIB cells would be possible by increasing the thickness of electrodes through decreasing the inactive materials fraction from 44 to 12 wt% and increasing the active materials load to over 30 wt%. as shown in Fig. 1(a) & (b) , where conventional and prospective LIB cell design with thicker electrode is depicted respectively [25].

An empirical model has been developed to represent the relationship between the energy density and electrode thickness, where the enhancement of energy density has been demonstrated to be more than 20 % for thicker electrodes with cathode thicknesses of NMC varying from 60 μm to 1000 μm [26]. The similar trend of enhancing energy density in thicker electrodes has been observed, but with deteriorated thermal characteristics [27].

A full order P2D model and a SPM are presented with assumptions and governing equations, including the discussion regarding other reduced order models that are used for computational analysis of LIB cells have been briefly presented in this section.

The P2D model, which is also known as Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model, was first introduced by Newman and his co-workers in 1990s. As mentioned earlier, two theories, one is related to concentrated solution and another is related to porous electrode, are the basis of the P2D model. In addition, the model incorporates reaction kinetics, Ohm's law, charge balance, and mass balance. The P2D model takes both solid and electrolyte phases into consideration, encompassing large number of internal cell parameters, and thereby, producing more accurate results of electrochemical reactions that happen inside the cell but with computational cost [28]. This model is a generic model to incorporate further understanding of battery systems as well as to lead to the formulation of numerous similar models. Coupled nonlinear partial differential equations in spatial coordinate x, radial coordinate r and time t are used to represent the electrochemistry, the principles of transport phenomena, and thermodynamics in this model [12]. The nonlinear equations are used to describe the conservation of mass and charge in both solid and liquid phase, and also the Butler-Volmer equation is used to represent the electrochemical kinetics of the cell [14]. The complex electrochemical phenomena that occur inside a LIB cell can be expressed through the aspects stated below along with representational equations of the electrochemical reactions: (1) charge balance equation-electronic, (2) charge balance equation-ionic in the electrolyte phase, (3) the diffusion of active particles in the solid phase with Fick's second law for the spherical particles, (4) charge transport-ionic in the electrolyte phase, and (5) reaction kinetics expressed by Butler-Volmer equation, representing the pore wall Li-ion flux in the electrodes. Potential in the solid phase is governed by the Ohm's law, and potential in the liquid phase is derived by both Ohm's law and Kirchhoff's law [13,29]. The prime equations and state variables of P2D model are associated with mass and charge conservations in solid active materials phase described by: active-materials Li-ion concentration
c s ±
(
x , r , t
)
and electric potential
∅ s
(
x , t
)
, mass and charge conservations of electrolyte phase given by: Li-ion concentration in the electrolyte phase
C l
(
x , t
)
and electric potential
∅ s
(
x , t
)
, solid-electrolyte interphase expressed by Butler-Volmer kinetics representing through: ionic currents
i s
(
x , t
)
and pore wall molar flux
j
(
x
,
t
)
, as well as materials balance [23].

The schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 2 and the governing equations for P2D model are presented below [13,17,19,30,31].

η = potential difference between the solid phase and the electrolyte phase. This potential difference can be expressed in terms of equilibrium potential, which is a function of SOC, and can be given as follows:
(10)
η =

Where,

= equilibrium potential (V),

and

= Solid and liquid phase potentials respectively. Finally, the cell voltage is calculated by the following equation:
(11)
V

∣
x = L
Where,
φ p
= Positive electrode potential at x = 0, and
φ n
=
Neagitve electrode potential at x = L.

The accuracy and robustness of P2D model is beyond the question, but the complicacy in applying the model is too high and the speed of application is too slow due to the computational burden resulted from lot of PDEs. To reduce the complexity and increase the speed of the P2D model, various reduced order models, including the SPM, have been explored by the researchers. The SPM is the most simplified form of physics-based electrochemical models, which makes the modelling approach simple by reducing the number of PDEs to one for each electrode and number of algebraic equations to one. In SPM, some assumptions are applied to simplify the PDEs associated with full order electrochemical modelling as described above. Two primary assumptions of SPM are: firstly, electrodes are regarded as separate spherical particles, one particle for each electrode, where intercalation-deintercalation occur i.e., same behaviour is considered from all the particles in an electrode, and secondly, ionic concentration and potential variations in the electrodes are ignored i.e., all the particles in an electrode receive uniform distribution of current. Thirdly, the concentration and the current in the electrolyte remain constant i.e., the variation of electrolyte concentration and the potential change with respect to time and position are ignored. Moreover, the SPM is based on the approximations that the amount of current applied to the cell is small and the electrolyte conductivity is large. Though the effects of electrolyte properties are ignored for the sake of simpler transport phenomena, the thermal aspects that impact the Li-ion battery performance are taken into account in the SPM. Hence, among the PDEs (1) to (6) of the above P2D model, only PDE (6) is considered for each electrode, which yields to a total of two PDEs along with the algebraic equation (18). Still, the SPM is not valid for more than 1C rate, especially, for high energy cells. However, for thicker electrodes and higher discharge rates, the SPM must be adjusted in accordance with the electrolyte properties. The schematic diagram for SPM is illustrated in Fig. 3 and the representational equations of the model are given below [13,31–33].

The Li-ion concentration and pore wall molar flux in the SPM is considered independent of the spatial coordinate, x. Hence,
c s ±
(
x , r , t
)
in equation (6) becomes
c s ±
(
r , t
)
in equation (12) and the pore wall molar flux
j
(
x
,
t
)
becomes
j
( t )
in equation (15). This assumption renders to an approximation of uniform reaction across the cell thickness [23]. Additionally, the approximations of application of small current to the cell and large conductivity of electrolyte makes
∂ C
l
∂ t
= 0
and
∂ ∁
l
∂ Χ
= 0
[32]. Hence, the SPM derived from the P2D model based on the above assumptions and variables modifications, yielding the concentration of Li-ion in the solid particle of electrodes is governed by the following two PDEs of Fick's second law of diffusion:
i)
Fick's law of diffusion for both positive and negative electrode,

R f ±
= Solid-electrolyte interface layer film resistance, and reff = Reaction rate constant.

As the SPM is only valid for lower than 1C rates, reduced order electrochemical models are necessary to represent the electrochemical phenomena in an LIB cell at higher discharge rates because of the remarkable variations in Li-ion concentration and cell potential profiles. Moreover, modified versions and simplified forms of P2D model, which have been proven useful in numerous practical applications like online monitoring, parameter estimation etc., are a balance between the SPM and P2D full-order model, particularly for the automotive applications [13]. Researchers have proposed many reduced order physics-based electrochemical models to make those models robust, computationally efficient, and to increase their applicability. For example, Rahimian et al. proposed a reduced order physics-based isothermal LIB model by incorporating materials balances and solution phase charge into the SPM. The concentration distribution and the potential of electrolyte in the electrodes and separator were considered in that model. The final model was able to predict the cell voltage up to 5C discharge rate [34]. An extended SPM (ESPM) was proposed by Luo et al. by encompassing the effect of both potential concentration of electrolyte and reaction distribution (non-uniform) into the SPM. The ESP model could be applied up to 4c discharge rate with less than 0.55 % error and was applicable to dynamics and arbitrary loads as well as real-time simulations in EV applications [35]. Tanim et al. presented extended SPM involving temperature dependant parameters (ESPM-T) with electrolyte diffusion, which showed the validity for higher C-rates up to 12.5C with higher range of temperature [36]. Fan et al. proposed an extended SPM, a reduced order P2D model, by including electrolyte dynamic in the SPM with reduction of order for the diffusions in the liquid and solid phases. The results of the model demonstrated a good alignment with the experimental data under different conditions [15]. Al Gabalawy et al. developed a SPM three-parameter model along with the detailed description of P2D model and SPM. They studied different values of temperature-dependant parameter, reff to examine the impact of that parameter on the discharge voltage of the cell [31]. Recently, Wu et al. has proposed an improved reduce order electrochemical model (iROEM) with enhanced observability, higher model fidelity, and reduced computational complexity. The iROEM has been able to accurately predict the cell terminal operating voltage at higher C-rates of up to 4C [14]. More recently, Couto et al. presented a dimensionless reduced order electrochemical model, which is a dimensionless SPM extended model, to predict and optimize the energy density of a LFP half-cell [23].

Due to the lack of complete solution (analytical) of P2D electrochemical model, various numerical methods along with different approximation techniques like Finite Volume Method (FVM), Orthogonal projections, Orthogonal collocations, Finite Difference Method (FDM), Finite Element Method (FEM) etc. have been utilized for the estimation of model parameters. Among them, the FDM and the FEM are two widely used methods in commercially available software [13]. In case of model order reduction, Rahimian et al. used Polynomial Profile (PP) approximation [34], Luo et al. used non-uniform polynomial, parabolic profile and volume averaging technique [35], Tanim et al. used Integral Method Analysis (IMA) [36], Fan et al. used Galerkin projection method [15], Al Gabalawy et al. utilized single particle approximation [31], Wu et al. applied both Pade approximation and volume averaging technique [14], and Couto et al. employed FDM [23]. A list of the various ways of order reduction of the full electrochemical model that have been used by the researchers, along with a table of different techniques used, is available in Ref. [13].

The simple SPM cannot accurately predict the energy density of LIB cells and generally fails to represent energy density values at C-rates greater than C/2 [32]. Nevertheless, if the simple SPM is extended by encompassing the electrolyte dynamics, the SPMe shows validity up to 5C rate. A comparison between P2D full order model, SPM, and SPMe has been performed, where the results show that the SPM is reasonable accurate for up to 1C rate, on the other hand, the validity of the SPMe is maintained for up to 5C rate. Hence, though the SPMe model is comparatively simple, it is a physically significant model, which can represent a wide range of electrochemical phenomena in LIB cells. The energy density of a LFP cell has been presented as a function of thickness with the help of the SPMe model [23].

Reduction of model order of P2D electrochemical model is out of the scope of this work. However, as the simple SPM described in section 3.2.1 cannot represent the energy density of a LIB cell, a ROEM, which can be denoted an extended SPM, has been presented by incorporating both the concentration and the potential of electrolyte to predict the energy density values of NCM LIB cells at higher discharge rates particularly, at higher than 0.5C rate. To achieve this, the reduced order P2D model presented by Wu et al. by applying both volume averaging technique (VAT) in case of simplification of electrolyte dynamic and quadratic polynomial profile (PP) approach to calculate the surface concentration of positive electrode is adopted here [14]. Finally, the blend of the SPM described in section 3.2.1 and the P2D model order reduction based on VAT and quadratic PP has contributed to a unique modelling approach, which is different from other existing ROEMs. The ROEM has then been used in this study to show the straightforward relationship between energy density and electrode thickness of a NCM LIB cell, which is another novelty of the proposed ROEM compared to the existing ones. Therefore, the proposed model consists of the combination of equation (12) for solid phase diffusion and concentration, along with the boundary conditions given in equation (13) -(16), equation (20) -(22) for open circuit potentials and stoichiometry, and the following equation (23) -(32) for electrolyte dynamics, surface concentration, total pore wall flux, including terminal cell output voltage.
i)

Hence, considering the concentration and the potential of electrolyte that were given in equations 4-01) - (4-07, the overall cell output voltage for the SPM in section 3.2.1 expressed in equations 3–17 & (3-18) becomes as follows.

It can be observed from equations 4–08 that the output voltage depends on the values of the surface concentration of Li-ions of the electrodes

Again, the surface concentration of both electrodes,
C

±
( t )
is expressed in equations 3–19, which can be obtained from the solution of equation (12) for Li-ion concentration in the solid particles. The open circuit potentials,
U p
and
U n
are related to the utilization rate of electrodes [14,31], both of which can be obtained from the polynomial equations given in equations (20) and (21). On the other hand, the assumption of solid phase potential as a function of time alone in the SPM can still be valid for the extended SPM for up to 5C rate [34]. Moreover, the exchange current density
i
0 ,

&

0 ,

for positive and negative electrodes can be considered as a function of pore wall molar flux,
j
( t )
and applied current,
i
a p p
. As the applied current is known, the exchange current density depends on the calculation of the value of the molar flux. Furthermore, the molar flux is also important to know for solving the PDEs in equation (12) due to being a boundary condition parameter [32]. The pore wall flux at interior points of both electrodes’ changes with respect to time during charge-discharge process. However, the total flux remains constant with times for a fixed applied current and can be calculated for each electrode as follows [34]:

Finally, the energy density of the cell can be predicted through the equation below.
iv)
The energy density of a Li-ion cell is given by,
(33)

Where,
M =
Mass of the LIB cell,
V
( t )
=
Terminal output voltage, and
i
a p p
=
Applied current.

The ROEM, described in section 4.1, has been implemented in MATLAB. A personal laptop having windows 11 Pro, version 22H2, 12 generation intel core-i7 with 16 GB RAM was used for running the simulation. The MATLAB academic version R2022b under the Central Queensland University campus-wide MATLAB licence has been downloaded in the above laptop computer to generate codes and run the program. To solve the PDEs in equation (12) along with initial and boundary conditions, the ‘pdepe’ solver in MATLAB has been used. The ‘pdepe’ solves PDEs in one spatial variable and time, t. Here, in the PDEs the spatial variable that need to be discretised is ‘r’, which indicates the radial distance of a particle of electrode materials. At the centre of the particle, the value of r is 0 and at the surface of the particle the value of r is denoted as Rp. Hence, the numerical solution of the PDEs returns the value of li-ion concentration throughout the particle from r = 0 to r = Rp. To discretise the PDEs in the spatial direction, the MATLAB PDE solver ‘pdepe’ uses FEM Method. Moreover, ‘pdepe’ utilises ‘ode15s’ algorithm for time integration, which can solve numerical differentiation formulas of order 1 to 5 and allows for the passing of extra parameters by defining them outside the function. The PDE solver can provide solutions for slab, cylindrical, and spherical symmetry taking the corresponding value of 0, 1, 2 for symmetry constant, m respectively. In this case, m = 1 is taken for solving the cylindrical symmetry of NMC cell. Other algebraic equations have also been coded and programmed in the same software as mentioned above to generate discharge voltage vs time plots at 0.05C, 1C, 2C, 3C, and 5C to compare the simulation results with the experimental ones to verify the model. More explanation about the results is included in the results and discussion section 6.
The input parameters used for the proposed ROEM are presented in Table 1 below.

Experimental data of LIBs, tested under various experimental conditions, is publicly available for conducting further research. For example, Reis et al. reviewed and summarized publicly available datasets under experimental test conditions and test variables to ease the ongoing research works [39]. The experimental data obtained from Stanford University was used in this study. The experiment was conducted by Energy Control Laboratory under Energy Resources Engineering Department. Their experimental set up with physical connections is shown in Fig. 4 and the experimental condition, along with data collection methodology, is described below.

The host computer, along with MITS Pro-software and Data Watcher, was used for programming the test profile and monitoring the real-time data as shown in Fig. 4(a). Moreover, the configuration of the input current is done in Fig. 4(a), which is then transmitted to the measuring instruments in Arbin measurement system shown in Fig. 4(b) and in Arbin LBT21024 in Fig. 4(c) through TCP/IP connection respectively. Voltage, surface temperature, and current of the cells were measured with the help of both measuring instruments. A temperature-controlled enclosure namely IncuMax IC-500R is used to locate test cell as shown in Fig. 4(d). A cylindrical cell holder, holding the test cell, with a thermocouple sensor positioned on the cell surface is shown in Fig. 4(e) [40].

The dataset is fully available online under the link provided by Catenaro et al. Three types of LIB cell chemistries namely nickel cobalt aluminium (NCA), NCM, and LFP were tested under 5 °C, 25 °C, and 35 °C by discharging the cells from the state of charge (SOC) of 100 % to the discharge cutoff voltage. Charging of the above-mentioned cells was performed by applying constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) protocols. Then, Galvano static discharge was applied to the cells with different ranges of discharge rates such as for NCA and NCM cells the range of discharge rates was from 0.2C to 5C while the range for LFP cells was from 0.2C to 20C [40]. However, this study used the data for NCM chemistry at 25 °C with Galvano static discharge from the above dataset. The discharge voltage vs discharge time graphs of selected experimental dataset at C-rates from C/20 to 5C for the chosen chemistry are illustrated in Fig. 5 (a)-(e), which are used for validating the proposed extended SPM.

It can be observed from the experimental graphs that the voltage drops increases at the end of the discharge. Although the voltage drop is a common discharge phenomenon, it shows a drastic drop at the later part of discharge. However, the rate of voltage drop depends on the rate of discharge. The sharpness in the voltage drop is observed at and around 3V and maintains the trend up to the discharge cutoff voltage of 2.5V at 0.05C, 1C, 2C, and 3C discharge rates. But at 5C rate, the drop is steeper, and the same trend is maintained throughout the discharge time, having higher cutoff voltage of 2.75V. Internal resistance of the cell, which increases with C rates, is the reason behind this voltage drops and its variation with respect to the C-rates.

Fig. 6 (a)–(e) compare simulation results from the proposed model described in section 4.1 with experimental data depicted in section 5. It is obvious from Fig. 6(a)–(c) that the proposed ROEM is highly compatible with the experimental data up to 2C discharge rate. Hence, the model is valid for C-rates up to 2C. However, Fig. 6(d) shows that the deviation between experiment and simulation results increases for higher C-rates such as at 3C. At 5C discharge rate, the lack of validity of the model is more conspicuous, which can be seen from Fig. 6(e). The results for model validation with experimental data using discharge voltage vs discharge time is similar to that of the published literature [27,41].

The proposed model under predicts the voltages at the time higher than 600 s and 200 s at 3C and 5C discharge rates respectively, which is obvious from Fig. 6(d)–(e). Because of the increased deviations between experiment and simulation results, starting from 3C discharge rate, the model was not regarded as valid beyond 2C discharge rate. The verification of the model has been done at cathode thickness of 75 μm, anode thickness of 50 μm, and electrode porosity of 0.4. The rest of the paper analysed the effects of electrode (cathode) thickness on energy density for C-rates up to 2C.

After validating the model, cathode thickness was varied from 75 μm to 600 μm to observe the effect of the variation of thickness on the cell energy density (see Fig. 7). For the initial cathode thickness of 75 μm, the energy density of the cell at C-rates of C/5, C/2, 1C, and 2C are shown in Fig. 7(a). Then, the thickness was gradually increased with multiplying factors of 2, 4, 6, and 8 to achieve cathode thickness of 150 μm, 300 μm, 450 μm, and 600 μm. The ROEM has predicted the values of energy density as illustrated in Fig. 7(b)–(e). The reason behind the range of cathode thickness assumed here is the concept of thicker electrode design for higher energy density of LIBs. It can be observed from the previous studies that the rate of energy density increase is higher in the initial stage of cathode thickness increment. The rate gradually decreases after a certain value of electrode thickness and then it almost disappears after 600 μm of thickness [26].

The value of energy density has improved from around 250 Whkg−1 at cathode thickness 75 μm (Fig. 7(a)) to around 300 Whkg−1 at 600 μm of cathode thickness (Fig. 7(e)), with a total of 20 % increase in energy density. However, the rate of energy density improvement is more in the early stage of cathode thickness increment than that of later within the selected range of thickness, resulting a lucrative increase in energy density value up to the cathode thickness of 300 μm. For the rest of the thickness increment from 300 μm to 600 μm, the rate of improvement in energy density is less, but still shows promising results. For example, energy density values increase by 8.1 % for cathode thickness from 75 μm to 150 μm, by 4.69 % for 150 μm–300 μm, by 2.99 % for 300 μm–450 μm and 2.01 % for 450 μm–600 μm respectively at C/2. This trend of increase in energy density indicates that it does not keep going up forever with the increase of electrode thickness and the largest value of cathode thickness lies somewhere between 500 and 600 μm for NCM cells. The improved energy density is attributed to the reduction of weight contribution from inactive materials [22,26] and the reason for decreased rate of energy density enhancement is the reduced discharge capacity at thicker electrode resulting from lower diffusion of Li-ions into the cathode, which acts as a limiting factor for reaction rate, particularly at higher C-rates [7]. The dramatic fall in the energy density observed at and around 3V, which has been maintained until the discharge cutoff voltage of 2.5V, is due to the lower utilization of energy form the cell at lower SOCs [32].

However, thicker electrode design is associated with some issues such as lower mechanical stability, slower charge kinetics, delamination of active materials, and enhanced charge transfer resistance etc. that need to be addressed before the benefits of high energy density is harnessed [25]. Moreover, the amount of total heat generation, resulting from drastic upsurge of ohmic heat and steady reversible heat, is more in thicker electrodes leading to the increase in maximum temperature of the cell, which necessitates a more sophisticated cooling system [27]. Furthermore, the power density, which is always regarded as a trade-off with energy density, of a LIB cell remarkably decreases with the increase in energy density from increment thickness of electrodes [20].

The above results (Fig. 7(a)–7(e)) can be summarized as illustrated in Fig. 8 , which shows the relationship between increase in energy density and cathode thickness of the LIB cell. It can be observed from the relationship that the rate of energy density increase is the highest at the earlier stage of cathode thickness increase of up to 300 μm. After 300 μm, the rate of energy density increase becomes moderate with the increase in cathode thickness. Moreover, the rate of energy density enhancement keeps dwindling along with further increase in cathode thickness, which implies that continuous increment of electrode thickness after a certain level does not bring any significant change in energy density. Therefore, there is a clear indication of an optimized value of cathode thickness above which pursuing thicker electrode is meaningless.

The results of enhanced energy density with respect to the increasing cathode thickness are like the outcomes of Du et al. Wood et al., and Xing et al. [24,26,42]. Nevertheless, it should be noted that electrochemical performance of a LIB cell is significantly influenced by several other design parameters such as porosity and tortuosity of electrode, electrolyte type, particle size distribution, and additive and binder type, amount etc. apart from electrode thickness [20].

This work has presented a novel modelling approach through gathering the review of existing literature regarding correlation between electrode thickness and energy density of LIB cells, describing the rigorous electrochemical model, along with reduced-order electrochemical models, including model order reduction techniques, proposing a novel extended SPM based on both the SPM and the P2D model order reduction methods in existing literature, performing numerical simulation in MATLAB to present a unique way of the direct relationship between energy density and thickness of a NCM cell, which is rare in the currently available research works. Hence, the proposed ROEM is different from P2D model in terms of reduced complexity and easiness of model execution. Moreover, the ROEM has better applicability than other reduced order models, that are described in section 2, including SPMe, iROEM etc., in terms of capturing energy density directly along with intermediate stages while producing similar results with much better predictability of the trend of energy density increment in LIBs with thicker electrodes.

In this paper, a reduced order simplified P2D model, which is an extended SPM model, has been presented and implemented in MATLAB to replicate the cell level energy density of LIBs. Simulation results demonstrate positive effects of electrode thickness on the energy density of the cells due to the decrease in weight contribution from inactive materials of LIBs. While a total of 20 % increase in energy density values has been illustrated with the increment of cathode thickness from 75 μm to 600 μm in an NCM cell, the rate of energy density improvement becomes slower with the increase in thickness particularly, after around 300 μm of cathode thickness. This trend indicates that continuous pursuing of thicker electrodes after a certain optimum value of thickness will not bring fruitful results. Hence, an optimum value for each cell chemistry based on their usage is important. The proposed model can predict energy density values up to 2C discharge rates satisfactorily. To produce valid simulation results for higher C-rates (higher than 2C) a more comprehensive model, which includes thermal characteristics and related influential parameters of the cell such as particle size distribution, tortuosity of the electrode etc., is necessary. Finally, to utilize the benefits of higher energy density from thicker electrodes, associated issues like mechanical instability, increased charge transfer resistance, lower charge kinetics etc. need to be resolved.

F.M. NizamUddin Khan: Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft, Investigation. Mohammad G. Rasul: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. A.S.M. Sayem: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Nirmal K. Mandal: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:F M Nizam Uddin Khan reports financial support was provided by Fund for Accessing Potential Expertise (FAPEx)-2022. A. S. M. Sayem reports a relationship with Fund for Accessing Potential Expertise (FAPEx-2022 that includes: funding grants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge and thank Fund for Accessing Potential Expertise (FAPEx) for providing financial support as FAPEx 2022 grant under the title “Design and optimization of lithium-ion battery as an efficient energy storage device for electric vehicles” to support this research.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure, modification, and commercialization of high nickel ternary material (LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 and LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2) for lithium ion batteries

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Framework for energy storage selection to design the next generation of electrified military vehicles
